coredata.dc:title: Sonochimical synthesis, characterization and physicochemical properties of Cu3Mo2O9 graphene-based nanocomposites for antibacterial therapeutic agent with enhanced activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Karkeh-Abadi, Fatemeh
Soofivand, Faezeh
Safardoust-Hojaghan, Hossein
Yousif, Qahtan A.
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: In this study, Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles (NPs) were synthesized by ultrasonic technique as a facile method. The effective parameters on properties of products including: sonication time, capping agent and calcination temperature were changed in order to prepare the desired properties including fine particles with homogeny morphologies. The nanoparticles synthesized in the optimum conditions were utilized to prepare graphene quantum dots-based nanocomposites (Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs). Next, antibacterial activities of the prepared products were studied. According to the results, the antibacterial performance of the nanocomposite was better than pristine nanoparticles. It can be interpreted through two mechanisms that both of them are related to the presence of graphene quantum dots: (a) increasing the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and (b) increasing the surface area that increases aggregation of nanoparticles on cell walls of bacteria. According to the results, the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and zone of inhibition of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs were recorded 156.25 μg/ml and 27 mm against Staphylococcus saprophyticu, respectively. Consequently, the prepared nanocomposite can be identified as a promising antibacterial product.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Ultrasonic
Antibacterial properties
Nanostructures
Cu3Mo2O9

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a, b): sample 1 and (c, d): sample 2.
XRD patterns of (a): sample 1, (b): sample 2 and (c): sample 3.
SEM images of (a): sample 2, (b): sample 4 and (c): sample 5.
XRD pattern of (a): sample 2 (Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles), and (b) sample 6 (Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposite).
FT-IR spectrum of (a): sample 2 and (b): sample 6.
(a, b): TEM images of sample 2 and (c, d): HRTEM images of sample 6 (nanocomposite).
Schematic of the formation of Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposites.
EDS mapping and EDS point analysis of Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles (sample 2).
EDS mapping and EDS point analysis of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposites (sample 6).

Disk diffusion test of Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles against different bacterial strains: methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumonia, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Staphylococcus saprophyticus, Shigella flexneri, Salmonella, Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

Disk diffusion test of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposites against different bacterial strains: methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumonia, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Staphylococcus saprophyticus, Shigella flexneri, Salmonella, Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
Time-kill curve using 0.5, and 1MIC of Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposites against methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA).
The MIC and MBC of the Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposites against tested microorganisms.
The inhibition zones (mm) of Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposites against tested microorganisms.

head.ce:title: Sonochimical synthesis, characterization and physicochemical properties of Cu3Mo2O9 graphene-based nanocomposites for antibacterial therapeutic agent with enhanced activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials, College of Engineering, University of Al-Qadisiyah, Diwaniyah, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials, College of Engineering, University of Al-Qadisiyah, Diwaniyah, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles (NPs) were synthesized by ultrasonic technique as a facile method. The effective parameters on properties of products including: sonication time, capping agent and calcination temperature were changed in order to prepare the desired properties including fine particles with homogeny morphologies. The nanoparticles synthesized in the optimum conditions were utilized to prepare graphene quantum dots-based nanocomposites (Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs). Next, antibacterial activities of the prepared products were studied. According to the results, the antibacterial performance of the nanocomposite was better than pristine nanoparticles. It can be interpreted through two mechanisms that both of them are related to the presence of graphene quantum dots: (a) increasing the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and (b) increasing the surface area that increases aggregation of nanoparticles on cell walls of bacteria. According to the results, the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and zone of inhibition of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs were recorded 156.25 μg/ml and 27 mm against Staphylococcus saprophyticu, respectively. Consequently, the prepared nanocomposite can be identified as a promising antibacterial product.

ce:section.ce:para: Infection induced by microorganisms can be considered as one of the agents that increases the need to intensive care services and mortality. Antibiotics are the most popular materials to treat infections which are used for consecutive years [1–3]. Although, the studies showed that using the excessive amount of antibiotic causes the proliferation of super-bacteria that are immune to the most antibiotics (multidrug-resistant bacterial strains) [4,5]. Necessity of appearance of compounds with antimicrobial activities, in addition to antibiotics, cause to propel the researches to the nanoscale [6–9]. Nanomaterials are used in various fields due to the unique properties that are different with bulk scale. Emerging these differences is induced by high ratio of surface to volume and quantum effects. In addition to, the properties of nanomaterials depend on morphologies and sizes of them that can be controlled by changing the synthesis method, the used reagents and the other effective parameters [10–15].

Mo-containing nanostructures (MCNs) due to having antibacterial properties and fascinating characteristics such as high chemical and thermal stability, biocompatibility, optical properties, and high decomposition temperature have received much attention [16–19]. According to that said about importance of size and morphology in nanomaterials, antibacterial activities of the different morphologies (0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D structures) of MCN compounds in many works have been done [20].

Nakajima and co-workers [18] investigated antibacterial performances of MoO3, La2O3, cerium (Ce) and tungsten-doped La2Mo2O9 nanomaterials against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. They applied the polymerization complex method to prepare the nanoparticles with size range 50–100 nm. They found that (Ce)-substituted and tungsten (W)-substituted La2Mo2O9 can be considered as promising materials with antibacterial activities against S. aureus and E. coli. In [21] the authors used the hydrothermal method for engineering ZnMoO4 nanomaterials in different sizes and crystallographic structures (α, β, and α/β phases). The results confirmed that the antibacterial performance of ZnMoO4 nanomaterials against E. coli depends on the size and crystallographic structure of it. Camila Cristina De Foggi and co-workers studied antibacterial properties of various sizes and morphologies of β-Ag2MoO4 which synthesized by co-precipitation method [22]. According to the results, solvent type was considered as an effective parameter on morphology of the products and high antibacterial activity of this compound against E. coli and Methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) was confirmed.

Zhenhao Xia et al. applied theoretical and experimental methods to study the antimicrobial activities of copper molybdate nanorod and silver molybdate layered nanostructures against gram-negative bacteria E. coli. They reported the bacteriostatic ring size as a criterion for antibacterial activity, so that the inhibition zones of Ag2MoO4 and Cu3Mo2O9 were measured 10.826 and 10.138 mm, respectively. The results showed the inhibition effect of Ag2MoO4 is stronger than Cu3Mo2O9 [16].

There are different challenges for preparation of MCNs including high-cost, broad size-distribution, and environmental considerations that affect the feasibility of antibacterial tests, unfortunately. Our research team has tried to identify the processes that facile the synthesize procedure of various MCNs, experimentally. So, different methods were used and various parameters were changed in order to prepare these compounds. Although, copper molybdate as a member of this family has been synthesized by different methods up to now.

Graphene quantum dots as one of the carbon allotropes, which are considered as nanoparticles of graphene, have received much attention because of interesting physical, chemical properties, recently. In comparison to graphene, GQDs can have more application in biomedical field because of they have the smaller size, less toxicity and better biocompatibility. The organic molecules and biomolecules can be conjugated to it through the ᴨ- ᴨ stacking interaction or electrostatic binding that how to connect depends on functional group type on surface of it. The monoatomic layer planar conjugate structure, the small size, large surface and functional groups that exist on its surface cause loading and anchoring of GQD by various materials such as: drugs, nanoparticles and gens [23].

In this work, it was synthesized Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles using sonication technique and to investigate the effect of graphene quantum dots on properties of these nanoparticles, Cu3Mo2O9/graphene quantum dots-based nanocomposite was prepared by hydrothermal method. Finally, antibacterial activities of the products as a biological application were investigated. The results confirmed the antibacterial properties of these products against different microorganisms.

A Philips-X'pertpro X-ray diffractometer was used to analyze XRD patterns using Ni-filtered Cu K radiation. FT-IR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet Magna-550 spectrometer in KBr pellets. The LEO-1455VP scanning electron microscope, which was fitted with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, was utilized to examine the shape and size of samples. The transmission electron microscope was applied to investigate the morphology-related features in further detail. All of the applied materials used in this study were analytical grade: Cu(NO3)2.6H2O (99.9%), Sodium dodecyl sulfate, Na2MoO4.2H2O, Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), Citric acid from Merck.

In typical procedure, 1 g of Cu(NO3)2.6H2O dissolved in distilled water was added to a surfactant solution consisting of SDS or PVP. The molar ratio of the copper salt to surfactant was considered as 1:1. Another solution was prepared by dissolving 0.69 g of Na2MoO4.2H2O in 50 ml of distilled water under stirring. The molar ratio of Cu: Mo was kept at 1:1. The molybdenum-containing sample was added to the copper-containing solution dropwise under ultrasonic irradiation with frequency and power of 40 kHz and 160 W respectively. The as-obtained precipitates were collected and washed with distilled water and ethanol several times. Finally, after drying the samples at 60 °C for 24 h, the obtained powders were calcined at 500 °C for 2 h. The synthesis conditions for different samples were given in Table 1 .

The GQDs were prepared according to that reported previously, with minor modifications. Briefly, in 25 ml water, citric acid (CA) was dissolved under vigorous stirring. The prepared solution was then transferred to a stainless autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. Then, the by-products were removed by centrifuging for 20 min at 12,000 rpm. The prepared GQDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further experiments.
For preparation of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs, the prepared Cu3Mo2O9 NPs were dispersed in GQDs solution under intense stirring for 36 h. Then, the solid was filtered and dried at ambient conditions.

The antibacterial activity of Cu3Mo2O9 and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanostructures were investigated against Methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA), S. aureus, Enterococcus faecalis, Klebsiella pneumonia, E. coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus epidermidis Staphylococcus saprophyticus, Shigella flexneri, Salmonella. For determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), Mueller Hinton broth with a two-fold dosage of Cu3Mo2O9 and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs were applied to wells. Mixtures for bacterium were diluted at a concentration of 5 × 105 CFU/ml, equivalent to a 0.5 McFarland norm. The samples were stored at 37 °C for 19 h. The MIC of Cu3Mo2O9 and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs that prohibited bacterial growth was measured. The minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC), and agar disk-diffusion tests were carried out completely following our previously published paper [24]. A Time-kill curve was used to study the bacterial reduction number which was incubated with as-obtained nanomaterials. The bacterial inoculums were adjusted to 106 CFU/ml. The experiments were carried out in Muller Hinton broth for 12 h via 0.5 × MIC, 1 × MIC of the Cu3Mo2O9 and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs against MRSA. Specimens (0.1 ml) were tested at 0, 4, 8, 12, and 24 h and aliquot onto BHI agar plates. The number of colonies on the BHI samples were determined in CFU/mL after treatment for 24 h at 37 °C.

As mentioned in the introduction section, antibacterial activities of nanomaterials depend on morphology and size of them. So, it was tried to control size and morphology of products by changing the effective parameters on them. Different analyses were used to characterize the as-prepared samples.

Using surfactants to control the morphology and size of nanomaterials have developed in recent decade. Surfactants prevent the growth and agglomeration of nanostructures by electrostatic repulsion (such as SDS) or steric hindrance (for example PVP). Capping agents can be considered as surfactants with massive structure surrounding the nanostructures.

SEM analysis is used to study the morphological characteristics of the as-prepared products. SEM images of samples synthesized using SDS (sample 1) and PVP (sample 2) were displayed in Fig. 1 (a, b) and (c, d), respectively. As shown, homogeneity of sample synthesized by PVP is more than sample prepared using SDS. It seems that the high energy of fine particles causes to aggregate the particles and produce these nanostructures. The massive polymeric structure of PVP provided more steric hindrance in comparison to SDS, so homogenous structures were formed. The approximate size of particles in sample 2 was estimated about 50 nm.

To investigate the purity of products, the XRD patterns of samples 1 and 2 are illustrated in Fig. 2 a and b, respectively. As shown, the diffraction lines in Fig. 2a can be assigned to CuMoO4 (copper molybdate) with Oryhorhombic crystal structure (a = 7.6590 Å, b = 14.6130 Å, and c = 6.8750 Å) and JCPDS no. 00-024-0055 and CuO with JCDPS no. 00-0050,661 as impurity. In Fig. 2b, all of diffraction lines are accordance to Cu3Mo2O9 with JCPDS no. 00-024-0055 as favorite product, so, type of surfactant affected the purity of the prepared sample. The average grain size was measured 33.7 nm through the Scherer equation [25]. In XRD pattern of related to sample 3 (Fig. 2c) what prepared without calcination step, no crystalline structure was detectable and it indicated an amorphous peak, so calcination was considered as a key step for synthesis of pure copper molybdate.

As known ultrasound waves are used to synthesis of nanomaterials by applying intense shear force and instant cooling according to the mechanism reported in many works. Time sonication as a main parameter can impress the size of product; furthermore the arrangement of fine particles to form different morphologies can be affected. Three times 30, 45 and 60 min for applying the ultrasound waves during the synthesis process were considered and the SEM images of samples were illustrated in Fig. 3 a-c, respectively. It seems that increasing the time sonication increasing particle size of product. Agglomeration of particles occurs due to collision of fine particles formed under sonication in various directions. Increasing the reaction time increased the possibility of growth of particles. On the other words, the more time formed the larger products. The preparation mechanism of Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles under sonication process was written as follows:

First, the Cu2+ and MoO4 2− ions were obtained from dissolving Cu(NO3)2.6H2O and Na2MoO4.2H2O in distilled water [29]

After synthesis of Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles under optimum conditions, these nanoparticles were used for preparation of nanocomposite based on GQD.

Fig. 4 represents the XRD pattern of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs which indicated the crystalline structure of Cu3Mo2O9 and GQDs, simultaneously. It is well known that GQDs have a peak in the range of 20–25° in the XRD pattern which can be overlapped with Cu3Mo2O9 peak at 20-25° range. Different intensities and width of peaks of composite compared to the pure Cu3Mo2O9 is due to the presence of graphene quantum dots.

FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared Cu3Mo2O9 NPs and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs have been presented in Fig. 5 a and b., respectively. The peaks at 420-530 cm−1 are attributed to Cu–O bonds. The peaks at 700-1000 cm−1 are related to the three components of Mo–O asymmetric stretching mode (F2, triple degenerated). C–C-related peaks in the range of 1100–1400 cm−1 confirm the linking of the GQDs on Cu3Mo2O9 NPs.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) imaging generates a highly-magnified image and it can be used to determine the accurate size of nanomaterials. TEM images of sample 2 was given in Fig. 6 a and b in two different scales. As shown, nanometric particles with mean size about 60 nm were formed when sample was synthesized in presence of PVP. This result was accordance to that estimated in SEM images. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image as one of the imaging mode of TEM can be used to study the crystallographic structure of a sample at an atomic scale. High resolution of it provides valuable data about properties of crystalline materials in nanoscale. HRTEM of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposite was illustrated in Fig. 6c and d. HRTEM images show the formation of the tiny size of graphene quantum dots alongside Cu3Mo2O9 NPs which confirmed the results obtained from SEM images. High-resolution transmittance electron microscope image of graphene quantum dots shows the high crystallinity nature of graphene quantum dots with an interplanar spacing of 0.34 nm that coincide with (002) facet of graphitic carbon.

Fig. 7 shows a schematic for formation of Cu3Mo2O9 NPs and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs schematically. EDS mapping is applied for the chemical analysis of products. Fig. 8 relates to prepared Cu3Mo2O9 NPs in the presence of PVP with calcination (sample 2) which confirmed the presence of Cu, Mo, and oxygen elements in products. The presence of carbon in Fig. 9 attributes to the linking of GQDs to Cu3Mo2O9. It can be concluded that the distribution of GQDs has happened well in the Cu3Mo2O9 matrix [26].

The MIC and MBC were tested via the broth micro-dilution route and the findings are displayed in Table 1. Among the tested bacteria, S. saprophyticus and S. aureus showed the highest sensitivity because of MIC amounts versus two species of bacteria were obtained 156.25 μg/ml in presence of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs. According to the results given in Table 2 , MIC and MBC values are 312.5 μg/ml and 625 μg/ml for Cu3Mo2O9 NPs and these values for Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs are 156.25 μg/ml and 625 μg/ml, respectively, versus S. aureus. For the MRSA case, the MIC value for Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposite (156.25 μg/ml) was lower than MIC value for Cu3Mo2O9 NPs (312.5 μg/ml). Also, the bactericidal effect of both nanostructures, in this case, was the same (312.5 μg/ml). The higher antibacterial activity of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposite can be explained through different mechanisms such as: damaging the membrane cells by electrostatic interactions, perturbation of homeostasis, affecting the genome and signal transduction.

The presence of chemical groups with negative charge on surface of bacterial membrane, due to carboxylic acid groups in proteins at biological pH, causes the attraction the metal ions. When the metal ions are absorbed on the surface of membrane can entry to bacterial cells and causes to toxicity of microorganisms, finally. Since, absorb procedure depends on surface area, so increasing the surface area can improve the attraction process and agglomeration of them cause damage to membranes. Because the nano-metal ions have high surface area, the attraction process was simply done, so they can represent better antibacterial properties compared to bulk-scale. Besides, entrance of nano-cations to the cells through the nanometric pores of the bacterial membranes is accelerated. Eventually, disorganization of cells occurs quickly [27]. The amount of MIC and MBC for Cu3Mo2O9 and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs against E. faecalis was the same and equal to 625 and 1250 μg/ml, respectively. For the S. epidermidis, the inhibitory effect of samples was the same (156.25 μg/ml), but the bactericidal effect of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs were higher than Cu3Mo2O9 NPs. Cu3Mo2O9 NPs have no inhibitory and bactericidal effects on the P. aeruginosa. In the S. flexneri case, Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs showed completely better inhibitory (625 μg/ml) and bactericidal (625 μg/ml) effects than Cu3Mo2O9 (2500 μg/ml).

A Time-kill curve was used to further investigate the antibacterial activity of synthesized nanostructures against MRSA. Time-kill curves of prepared nanomaterials have been presented in Fig. 12 . It was found that in all three nanostructures, increasing the concentration of nanostructures leads to a faster reduction in the number of MRSA. It was clear that unlike Cu3Mo2O9 when Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs were applied all MRSA are destroyed after 24 h. In general, it was concluded that Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs destroy bacteria faster than Cu3Mo2O9 NPs.

This work was done in three steps including synthesis of Cu3Mo2O9 nanoparticles by sonication technique at the first step, preparation of Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs nanocomposite through hydrothermal method in the second step, and investigation of antibacterial properties in the third step. At initial examinations, type of surfactant and time of sonication were considered as two effective variables on morphology and purity of products, respectively. Despite role of surfactant as controlling the size and morphology, the results showed that surfactant type can be affected the purity of product. On the other hands, increasing the steric hindrance decreased the size of products as expected. In continue, it was found that calcination is a necessary parameter for obtaining the pure product with high crystallinity. Then, the antibacterial activities of Cu3Mo2O9 NPs and Cu3Mo2O9/GQDs NCs by using a wide range of bacteria were studied and their performances were compared together. As expected, the antibacterial properties of the prepared nanocomposite were more than the Cu3Mo2O9 NPs, it can be explained by increasing the surface area of nanocomposite that induced by presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Increasing the surface increases aggregation of nanocomposites on the surface of cell walls of bacteria and it is facilitated destruction of cells. One of the other reasons for the better performance of nanocomposite compared to pristine nanoparticles was production of more ROS species that can destroy bacteria. It should be noted that facile synthesis and antibacterial properties of this compound can be considered as some of advantages of this work.

To summarize which was resulted in this work, the interesting cases were noted as follows:
•
Importance of calcination process for obtaining the pure product.
•
Affecting the capping agents on purity of products by and investigation of their roles on controlling the morphology and size.
•
The acceptable results for antibacterial properties of two products.
•
The better performance of nanocomposite as an antibacterial agent because of providing the more surface.
•
Improving the antibacterial activity of nanoparticles by increasing the ROS species and facilitation of the electron–hole photo-production.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF, 97017837) and the University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/FK1).

Y. Wang, Y. Yang, Y. Shi, H. Song, and C. Yu, Advanced Materials, 2020, 32(18), 1904106.
J. Jutkina, N. P. Marathe, C. F. Flach, and D. G. J. Larsson, Science of The Total Environment, 2018, 616-617, 172-178.

W. Chin, G. Zhong, Q. Pu, C. Yang, W. Lou, P. F. De Sessions, B. Periaswamy, A. Lee, Z. C. Liang, X. Ding, S. Gao, C. W. Chu, S. Bianco, C. Bao, Y. W. Tong, W. Fan, M. Wu, J. L. Hedrick, and Y. Y. Yang, Nature Communications, 2018, 9(1), 917.
K. Guz-Regner, U. K. Komarnicka, B. Futoma-Koloch, M. Wernecki, M. Cal, S. Koziel, A. Ziolkowska, and G. Bugla-Ploskonska, Journal of Inorganic Biochemistry, 2020, 210, 111124.
S. Mickymaray, Biomolecules, 2019, 9(11), 662.
X. Yang, P. Xia, Y. Zhang, S. Lian, H. Li, G. Zhu, and P. Wang, ACS Applied Bio Materials, 2020, 3(8), 5395-5406.
N. H. Harun, R. B. S. M. N. Mydin, S. Sreekantan, K. A. Saharudin, N. Basiron, F. Aris, W. N. Wan Mohd Zain, and A. Seeni, ACS Omega, 2020, 5(21), 12027-12034.
S. H. El-Moslamy, Scientific Reports, 2018, 8(1), 3820.

M. Nasrollahzadeh, Z. Issaabadi, M. Sajjadi, S. M. Sajadi, and M. Atarod: 'Chapter 2 - Types of Nanostructures', in 'Interface Science and Technology', (eds. M. Nasrollahzadeh, et al.), 29-80; 2019, Elsevier.
Z. Xu, S. Mahalingam, J. L. Rohn, G. Ren, and M. Edirisinghe, Materials Science and Engineering: C, 2015, 56, 195-204.
M. A. Muflikhun, A. Y. Chua, and G. N. C. Santos, Advances in Materials Science and Engineering, 2019, 2019, 9821535.
J. T. Seil and T. J. Webster, Int J Nanomedicine, 2012, 7, 2767-2781.

R. C. de Oliveira, C. C. de Foggi, M. M. Teixeira, M. D. P. da Silva, M. Assis, E. M. Francisco, B. N. A. d. S. Pimentel, P. F. d. S. Pereira, C. E. Vergani, A. L. Machado, J. Andres, L. Gracia, and E. Longo, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, 2017, 9(13), 11472-11481.
